content.problemStatement:
Chef has a rectangular grid of size **R √ó C**, where each cell contains a certain number of chocolates. To collect chocolates efficiently, Chef asks for help from his two friends: **Alice** and **Bob**.


**Alice** starts at the **top-left cell** of the grid, position `(0, 0)`.
**Bob** starts at the **top-right cell** of the grid, position `(0, C ‚àí 1)`.
Both Alice and Bob move **from the top row to the bottom row**, one row at a time.
From a cell `(i, j)`, they can move to any of the following cells in the next row:


`(i + 1, j)`
`(i + 1, j ‚àí 1)`
`(i + 1, j + 1)`
Both friends must always remain within the grid boundaries.

Each friend collects all the chocolates from the cell they land on.
If **both Alice and Bob land on the same cell at any point**, the chocolates from that cell are **counted only once**.

Your task is to determine the **maximum total number of chocolates** that Chef can collect by choosing optimal paths for both Alice and Bob.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemaximumChocolatesmaximumChocolatesmaximumChocolates ‚Äì This function calculates the maximum number of chocolates that Chef can collect using two friends moving through the grid.

Parameters
RRR : Number of rows in the grid
CCC : Number of columns in the grid
gridgridgrid : A 2D array of size R√óCR \times CR√óC where grid[i][j]grid[i][j]grid[i][j] represents the number of chocolates in cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j)
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the **maximum total chocolates** collected.
Constraints
1‚â§T‚â§101 \leq T \leq 101‚â§T‚â§10
1‚â§R,C‚â§501 \leq R, C \leq 501‚â§R,C‚â§50
0‚â§grid[i][j]‚â§1000 \leq grid[i][j] \leq 1000‚â§grid[i][j]‚â§100
Alice always starts at (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0)
Bob always starts at (0,C‚àí1)(0, C ‚àí 1)(0,C‚àí1)
Chocolates from overlapping cells are counted only once

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer TTT ‚Äî the number of test cases




For each test case:



The first line contains two integers RRR and CCC
The next RRR lines each contain CCC space-separated integers representing the grid




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer ‚Äî the maximum number of chocolates that Chef can collect.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Alice starts at (0, 0) and Bob starts at (0, 2).
One optimal strategy:
Alice‚Äôs path:
(0,0) ‚Üí\rightarrow‚Üí (1,0) ‚Üí\rightarrow‚Üí (2,0)
Chocolates collected = 10 + 1 + 1 = 12
Bob‚Äôs path:
(0,2) ‚Üí\rightarrow‚Üí (1,2) ‚Üí\rightarrow‚Üí (2,2)
Chocolates collected = 10 + 1 + 1 = 12
Alice and Bob never land on the same cell.
Total chocolates collected
12 + 12 = 24

Explanation:
Alice starts at (0, 0) and Bob starts at (0, 1).
Optimal paths:
Alice's path:
(0,0) ‚Üí\rightarrow‚Üí (1,0)
Chocolates collected = 5 + 2 = 7
Bob's path:
(0,1) ‚Üí\rightarrow‚Üí (1,1)
Chocolates collected = 1 + 10 = 11
Both friends stay on different cells in each row.
Total chocolates collected:
7 + 11 = 18
